source.name: MDN SVG filter turbulence and displacement references
source.license_notes: Distilled implementation guidance from public SVG filter and Liquid Glass material principles. Store summarized guidance only.

visual_traits: slow refractive water-light caustics behind glass
thin flowing highlight veins that suggest optical distortion
subtle cyan, silver, and warm reflected light
liquid depth without turning the page into an aquarium
background movement that makes glass edges feel optically active
layout_rules: Use caustics as low-opacity environmental light, not as the main content texture.
Keep caustic intensity stronger near large glass surfaces and weaker under text-heavy regions.
Pair caustic backgrounds with fewer glass layers; too many translucent panels over moving light become noisy.
Use calm base surfaces behind forms and tables, with caustics limited to margins or empty zones.
Reserve sharp specular lines for edges, dividers, and primary glass controls.
accessibility_notes: Use reduced-motion to pause flowing caustics or replace them with a static texture.
Do not place validation text, table cells, or long paragraphs over bright caustic lines.
Keep caustic contrast below the contrast of interactive focus rings.
Add a tint layer behind glass cards when moving highlights reduce readability.
implementation_hints: SVG baseline: use feTurbulence as a procedural noise source and feDisplacementMap or masked gradients for slow refractive distortion.
Canvas baseline: render low-resolution flowing noise or line fields, upscale softly, and keep opacity low.
CSS fallback: layer radial gradients and thin blurred linear highlights with long transform animations.
Throttle intensity on mobile and avoid animating large blur/filter values every frame.
Use requestAnimationFrame for canvas animation and stop or pause when the page is hidden.
Keep caustic animation loops slow, around 24s to 90s, with no visible reset.
avoid_when: heavy SVG filters over full-screen layers on low-end devices

quality.notes: Pattern uses SVG turbulence/displacement and Liquid Glass optical-material reasoning to make glass backgrounds feel refractive while preserving legibility.

retrieval.ai_summary: Use water caustics when Liquid Glass needs a more optical, refractive background than a static gradient. Keep flowing light low-opacity, slow, and away from text; implement with SVG turbulence/displacement, low-res canvas noise, or CSS highlight layers, with reduced-motion and static fallbacks.
